question: [File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: '] [Function: normalizeRequestQueryParams()] Can an unprivileged external user inject a crafted query parameter value containing a literal '&name=' substring so that when getNormalizedArrayValue() calls valueArray.join('&' + name + '=') at line 78, the resulting normalized URL contains extra synthetic parameters, causing the downstream SQL query or cache lookup to operate on attacker-injected filter conditions?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: '] [Function: normalizeRequestQueryParams()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a query parameter whose value contains an unencoded '&' character so that the manual string concatenation at line 54 ('name + '=' + normalizedValue') produces a normalized URL with injected extra key=value pairs, bypassing any validation that was applied to the original parsed query object?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: '] [Function: normalizeRequestQueryParams()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a query parameter value containing '=' so that the concatenation at line 54 produces a malformed key=value=extra segment in the normalized URL, causing downstream parsers to misinterpret the parameter name or value and return data belonging to a different query context?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: '] [Function: getNormalizedArrayValue()] Can an unprivileged external user send a repeated query parameter (array) where one element contains the literal string '&timestamp=' so that the join at line 78 synthesizes an additional timestamp filter in the normalized URL, altering the SQL query result set to include or exclude records the attacker should not control?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: '] [Function: getNormalizedArrayValue()] Can an unprivileged external user send a repeated query parameter (array) where one element contains '&block.number=' so that the join at line 78 injects a synthetic block.number filter into the normalized URL, causing the API to return results from a different block range than the user legitimately requested?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: '] [Function: normalizeRequestQueryParams()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a request to a route that is absent from openApiMap so that the isEmpty(openApiParameters) branch at line 37-39 returns the raw query via querystring.stringify(query) without normalization, bypassing any default-value enforcement or parameter ordering that downstream logic depends on for correct SQL generation?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: '] [Function: normalizeRequestQueryParams()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that when openApiMap has no entry for the route, the raw query object is serialized with querystring.stringify(query) at line 38, which may produce a different encoding than the manual concatenation path, causing cache key divergence that allows a poisoned cache entry to be served to other users?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: '] [Function: normalizeRequestQueryParams()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger cache poisoning by crafting a request whose normalized URL (built via manual string concatenation at lines 54-58) matches the cache key of a different legitimate request, causing the server to serve the attacker's response to other users or serve another user's cached response to the attacker?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: '] [Function: getNormalizedArrayValue()] Can an unprivileged external user send an extremely large array of values for a non-COLLAPSABLE, non-NON_SORTED parameter so that the in-place valueArray.sort() at line 72 consumes excessive CPU time, causing a denial-of-service condition for all concurrent API users?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: '] [Function: getNormalizedArrayValue()] Can an unprivileged external user send thousands of repeated query parameter values for a sortable parameter so that the O(n log n) sort at line 72 combined with the join at line 78 produces an extremely long normalized URL string, exhausting memory or hitting URL-length limits in downstream components?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: '] [Function: normalizeRequestQueryParams()] Can an unprivileged external user send a large number of distinct query parameters that all map to openApiParameters entries so that the for-loop at lines 42-56 iterates over all of them and the appendToQuery calls at line 54 build an unbounded normalized query string, causing memory exhaustion or response-size amplification?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: '] [Function: getNormalizedArrayValue()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the COLLAPSABLE_PARAMS last-value-wins behavior at line 75 (valueArray.slice(valueArray.length - 1)) for the 'balance' filter key to override a previously validated or sanitized value with an attacker-controlled last value, causing the API to filter on an unintended balance value?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: '] [Function: getNormalizedArrayValue()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the COLLAPSABLE_PARAMS last-value-wins behavior at line 75 for the 'nonce' filter key to force the normalized URL to use only the attacker's chosen nonce value, discarding earlier values that may have been validated, and causing the API to return transaction data for a different nonce?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: '] [Function: getNormalizedArrayValue()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the COLLAPSABLE_PARAMS last-value-wins behavior at line 75 for the 'scheduled' filter key to override a legitimate filter value with an attacker-supplied last value, causing the API to return scheduled or unscheduled transactions contrary to what the original request intended?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: '] [Function: getNormalizedArrayValue()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the COLLAPSABLE_PARAMS last-value-wins behavior at line 75 for the 'block.hash' filter key to inject an attacker-controlled block hash as the effective filter, causing the API to return data for a block the attacker specifies rather than the one the legitimate user requested?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: '] [Function: normalizeRequestQueryParams()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that param.defaultValue at line 49 is appended to the normalized query without any sanitization or encoding, so that if a default value in the OpenAPI spec contains special characters like '&' or '=', the resulting normalized URL contains injected parameters that alter downstream query behavior?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: '] [Function: normalizeRequestQueryParams()] Can an unprivileged external user omit a query parameter entirely so that the default value branch at lines 48-50 injects param.defaultValue into the normalized URL, and if that default value is attacker-influenced (e.g., via a manipulated OpenAPI spec loaded at startup), it causes all requests missing that parameter to use the attacker's injected default?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: '] [Function: appendToQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a query parameter name (param.parameterName from openApiMap) that contains '&' or '=' characters so that the string concatenation at line 54 ('name + '=' + normalizedValue') produces a malformed query segment that injects additional parameters into the normalized URL?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: '] [Function: normalizeRequestQueryParams()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request where the query object contains a key with value 'undefined' (string) rather than the JavaScript undefined so that the check at line 46 ('value !== undefined') passes, and the string 'undefined' is appended to the normalized URL as a parameter value, causing unexpected downstream behavior?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: '] [Function: normalizeRequestQueryParams()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit prototype pollution on the query object (e.g., by sending a parameter named '__proto__' or 'constructor') so that the property access at line 44 ('query[name]') returns a polluted value, causing normalizeRequestQueryParams() to inject unexpected content into the normalized URL?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: '] [Function: normalizeRequestQueryParams()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request with a query parameter named 'toString' or 'valueOf' so that the property access query[name] at line 44 returns a function reference, and the subsequent isEmpty or string concatenation operations behave unexpectedly, causing a runtime error or unexpected normalized URL?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: '] [Function: getNormalizedArrayValue()] Can an unprivileged external user send an array value for a NON_SORTED_PARAMS parameter (e.g., timestamp) where one element contains '&timestamp=' so that the join at line 78 produces a normalized URL with a synthetic second timestamp filter, causing the SQL query to apply an attacker-controlled timestamp constraint?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: '] [Function: getNormalizedArrayValue()] Can an unprivileged external user send an array value for the 'block.number' parameter (a NON_SORTED_PARAMS member) where elements contain injected '&block.number=' substrings so that the join at line 78 synthesizes additional block.number filters, causing the API to return results from attacker-specified block ranges?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: '] [Function: normalizeRequestQueryParams()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request where the path argument contains query-string-like characters (e.g., '?foo=bar') so that the concatenation at line 58 ('path + '?' + normalizedQuery') produces a double-question-mark URL, causing downstream routing or parsing to misinterpret the path and potentially access unintended API endpoints?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: '] [Function: normalizeRequestQueryParams()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request where the path contains a '#' fragment identifier so that the concatenation at line 58 appends the normalized query after the fragment, causing the fragment to be treated as part of the query string by some downstream parsers and leaking or altering query semantics?
